title: Choosing test type for a multi-step checkout flow
scenario_context: E-commerce checkout: cart → address → shipping → payment → confirmation. Team has 40 E2E tests covering 'happy paths' that take 22 minutes in CI and flake ~8% of runs. Bugs still slip to prod (tax calc wrong for AK/HI, payment retry double-charges).
what_should_be_tested: Tax calculation rules per state (unit), address validation against USPS format (unit), payment retry idempotency (integration with stubbed Stripe), full checkout submit (1 E2E smoke). NOT every form-field combination via E2E.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: E2E for things that are pure-function business logic
Mocking Stripe in E2E so the test no longer validates the real integration shape
Single mega-test that fails opaquely
Asserting on DOM structure when behavior is what matters
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Tax math is a pure function of (lineItems, state, exemptions). Unit tests with table-driven cases give 50ms feedback vs 30s E2E.
2. Payment idempotency is the real bug source — needs integration test with stripe-mock, NOT E2E (browser adds no value, only flakiness).
3. The E2E pyramid here should be 1-2 smoke tests proving the wire-up works. Coverage of business rules lives below.
4. Real incidents: AK shipping bug was a tax-rule edge case shipped because E2E only ran 'CA, NY, TX'. Unit table-test with all 50 states + DC + territories would catch it in 80ms.
5. Fits pyramid: ~500 unit, ~50 integration, ~5 E2E. Current 40 E2E is upside-down.
mock_strategy: Tax: zero mocks — pure function. Payment integration test: stripe-mock (Stripe's official Docker image) to validate idempotency-key semantics — mocking @stripe/stripe-node would let bugs in our retry logic pass. E2E: real test Stripe account in restricted mode, 1 happy path only.
edge_cases_tested: Exempt purchaser overrides any state rate
Unknown state code throws — never silently defaults to 0
what_would_break_without_this_test: Real incident: a refactor changed `rates[state] ?? 0` and tax silently became $0 for any state not in the lookup. Caught by audit 3 months later, $340k in uncollected tax owed. The throw + table test would have caught it in CI.
ci_considerations: Tax suite: <100ms, no parallelism needed. Integration (stripe-mock): 15s startup amortized across suite, run in single CI job with --pool=forks. E2E smoke: 1 worker, retry=2, traces on failure.

title: Custom matcher for domain-specific money assertions
scenario_context: Finance app: every test asserts on Money objects ({amount: bigint, currency: string, scale: 2}). Tests are full of `expect(result.amount).toBe(1050n); expect(result.currency).toBe('USD')` repeated. Worse: some tests pass when amount is correct but currency is wrong because the second assert was forgotten.
what_should_be_tested: That a Money value equals an expected amount AND currency atomically. The matcher should fail clearly if either differs, and refuse to compare different currencies (catching bugs like adding USD+EUR).
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Partial assertions that pass when half the value is wrong
toEqual on objects with bigint (it works but error messages are unreadable)
Custom matcher that swallows the diff so failures say 'expected Money, got Money'
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Domain matchers turn 'expected 1050n, got 999n' into 'expected $10.50 USD, got $9.99 USD'. Readability is debugging speed.
2. The matcher enforces an invariant the type system can't: same-currency comparison. Mismatched currency is a bug, not a test expectation.
3. Custom matchers belong in `tests/matchers/` and are imported in `vitest.setup.ts` — not magically global, so future readers can find them.
4. Bug it prevents: USD/EUR mixup at a payments API boundary returned the right number with the wrong currency. Tests passed because nobody asserted currency.
5. Mutation-tests itself: changing `===` to `!==` in the matcher would surface immediately if the matcher is itself tested.
mock_strategy: No mocks. Money is a value object — pure function territory. The matcher itself has unit tests (matchers can have bugs too).
edge_cases_tested: Same amount, different currency → fails loudly
Same currency, different scale (USD vs JPY-style) → fails
what_would_break_without_this_test: Cross-currency bugs ship silently. Real incident: a fee calc returned `{amount: 100n, currency: 'EUR'}` instead of USD. Test asserted amount only, passed. Customer charged €100 instead of $100, ~$18 over. Multiplied across 4k transactions before detection.
ci_considerations: Matcher itself: <5ms. Adds zero runtime cost to suite. Imported once via setupFiles, no per-test overhead.

title: Testing a form by user behavior, not implementation
scenario_context: Signup form with email + password + confirm-password. Existing test imports useState internals, asserts state shape, breaks on every refactor. Real bug shipped: submit button enabled when passwords didn't match because validation ran on blur, not on change.
what_should_be_tested: Observable user behavior: typing mismatched passwords keeps submit disabled and shows the mismatch error; correcting them enables submit. Test must not know about internal state names or hook structure.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: getByTestId when getByRole works (testids couple to implementation)
Asserting on hook internals via renderHook when the component test would cover it
fireEvent.change instead of userEvent.type (fireEvent skips real keyboard semantics)
await waitFor with no assertion inside (silent passes)
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Tests should fail when behavior breaks, NOT when implementation changes. Querying by role/label survives refactors from useState→useReducer→react-hook-form.
2. userEvent.setup() reflects real keyboard/mouse semantics (keydown, keypress, input). fireEvent skips them, masking bugs in onKeyDown handlers.
3. The mismatch-validation-on-blur bug exists because the test asserted final state, never observed the intermediate. Real user types → expects feedback BEFORE blur.
4. getByRole('button', {name:/sign up/i}) is what a screen reader sees; if test can't find it, accessibility is broken too.
5. Bug caught: any change to validation timing immediately fails the test because we type, then check submit state, without blurring.
mock_strategy: onSubmit is a vi.fn() at the component boundary — that's the contract. No mocks for internal hooks, no mocks for validation library. Test the component the same way a user uses it.
edge_cases_tested: Mid-type mismatch is detected (not just on blur)
Correcting mismatch removes the error message
Submit fires with the exact values the user typed
what_would_break_without_this_test: The blur-vs-change validation bug shipped twice. With this test, refactoring validation timing immediately fails because we never call .blur() — proving validation reacts to typing, which is what users experience.
ci_considerations: ~80ms per test with jsdom. Parallel-safe (no shared state). userEvent.setup() per test ensures clean keyboard state — sharing causes phantom keypresses across tests.

title: Auth state reuse across E2E specs without flakiness
scenario_context: 50 E2E tests each call /login at the start, adding 4s × 50 = 200s of redundant auth work per CI run. Worse: login endpoint occasionally rate-limits in CI, causing 5% flake. Solution must reuse auth without leaking state across tests.
what_should_be_tested: That authenticated flows actually work when state is reused, AND that test isolation is preserved (test A logging out doesn't break test B). Login flow itself is tested ONCE separately.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Sharing a single browser context across tests (state leaks)
Calling /login in every beforeEach (slow + rate-limits)
Storing auth in env vars (stale tokens between runs)
Setting storageState globally and never refreshing it (tokens expire mid-suite)
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Playwright's storageState pattern: do auth ONCE in a setup project, save cookies/localStorage to disk, every test reuses it via use: {storageState}.
2. The setup project is a real Playwright project listed first in projects array with no dependencies. Other projects depend on it.
3. Token refresh: store the storageState in a file with a freshness check; if older than half-life, re-auth.
4. Test isolation: each test gets a FRESH browser context built from the saved storageState — not a shared one. State changes (logout, cart adds) don't bleed.
5. Login flow itself is tested in 1 spec, not reused — proves the auth path still works.
mock_strategy: No mocking at the browser layer — that defeats E2E purpose. The token lifetime check is real-system: we honor the auth service's actual TTL.
edge_cases_tested: First CI run: no saved state, performs full login
One test mutates cart; next test starts with empty cart (fresh context)
what_would_break_without_this_test: Without storageState reuse: 200s of CI wasted per run + flake from rate-limited login. With shared CONTEXT (not state) reuse: test A logs out → test B fails mysteriously. The freshness check prevents 'token expired mid-suite' flake at 39min mark.
ci_considerations: Setup project adds ~6s once, saves ~190s. Total speedup ~3min per run × 30 runs/day = 90min/day. trace:'on-first-retry' gives debug data without overhead on green runs.

title: Integration testing Express endpoints with real Postgres, not mocks
scenario_context: Order API: POST /orders inserts into orders + order_items in a transaction, debits inventory, emits an event. Existing tests mock pg, which means a missing FOREIGN KEY shipped to prod and orphaned 12k order_items.
what_should_be_tested: That the endpoint produces the right DB state (rows in both tables, inventory decremented) atomically, and that constraint violations roll back cleanly. With a REAL Postgres — not a mocked client.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Mocking pg.Pool so tests don't catch FK/constraint violations
Sharing a single DB across parallel test workers (interference)
Truncating after every test (slow); resetting via transaction rollback
Using Sequelize/Prisma sync() — masks migration bugs
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Mocking the DB makes tests fast and useless. Constraint violations, deadlocks, sequence overflow, FK cascades — none surface against a mock.
2. Use the SAME migrations CI/prod use. Apply them once per test run. Reset between tests via transactional rollback (each test runs inside a transaction that's aborted on cleanup) — 100x faster than TRUNCATE.
3. Parallel safety: each worker gets its own schema (search_path) inside a shared DB. Faster than per-worker DBs.
4. supertest drives the real Express app; no HTTP mocking. The endpoint goes through middleware, validation, handler, DB, response — the whole chain.
5. The orphan-rows bug: with a mocked client, `INSERT INTO order_items` succeeded even when orders insert failed. Real Postgres with proper FK + transaction catches it instantly.
mock_strategy: Database: real Postgres (Docker in CI). Event emitter: spy with vi.spyOn to assert it was called with the right payload AFTER commit — but the event bus implementation is real. External HTTP (payment gateway): MSW. The rule: mock the boundary you don't own, never the persistence layer.
edge_cases_tested: Multi-row insert atomicity (both tables or neither)
Inventory decrement is part of the same transaction
what_would_break_without_this_test: Real incident: a migration dropped the FK constraint accidentally. Mocked tests passed. Production orphaned 12k order_items rows in 6 hours before alerting fired. Real-DB integration test would have failed the moment the constraint was missing.
ci_considerations: Postgres in docker-compose, migrate once per CI job (~2s), tests run in transactions (~30ms each). Per-worker schemas allow parallelism without truncate-storms. Total: 200 integration tests in ~12s.

title: Property-based test for a billing proration function
scenario_context: SaaS proration: upgrading mid-cycle should charge prorated_amount = new_price × (days_left/cycle_days) - existing_credit. Example-based tests cover '15 days into 30-day cycle = half', missing edge cases. Real bug: cycle starting Feb 28 spanning a leap day produced negative proration.
what_should_be_tested: Invariants: proration never negative (refunds handled separately), proration ≤ new_price, proration = 0 on the last day of cycle, proration = (new_price - credit) on day 1. Across arbitrary dates, prices, cycles.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Property tests that generate random data without shrinking config (shrinking turns 200-line counter-examples into 3 lines)
Asserting weak invariants that pass trivially (proration >= -∞)
Using Math.random in 'property' tests (not reproducible)
Tests so slow they don't run in CI
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Example-based tests verify the cases YOU thought of. Property tests verify invariants the function must ALWAYS satisfy.
2. The leap-year bug is exactly what property testing finds: fc.date generates Feb 29 across multiple decades; example-based tests would never pick it.
3. Shrinking is the killer feature: when a property fails, fast-check shrinks to minimal repro — usually 1-3 line counter-example.
4. Seed-based determinism: fc.assert uses a seed printed on failure. Re-run with that seed reproduces the bug exactly.
5. Properties are SPEC: 'proration ≤ new_price' is a business rule. Tests double as executable documentation.
mock_strategy: Zero mocks — pure function. The test exercises the math across thousands of inputs, all reproducible from the seed fast-check prints on failure.
edge_cases_tested: Credit larger than prorated amount (output clamps to 0, not negative)
Rounding consistency at the cent boundary
what_would_break_without_this_test: The leap-day bug went undetected for 11 months because no example test included Feb 29. Property test would have caught it the first run with seed-based shrinking pointing at `{daysLeft: -1, ...}` from a date-math bug.
ci_considerations: 1000 runs × 5 properties ≈ 80ms. Seeds logged on failure for reproducibility. Run on every PR — cost is negligible, signal is high.

title: Using Stryker to find tests that don't actually test
scenario_context: Team has 89% line coverage and is proud of it. Stryker run reveals 41% mutation score — meaning 59% of code mutations don't fail any test. Tests are executing code but not asserting on its outputs.
what_should_be_tested: That changing operators (>, <, &&, ||), removing function bodies, flipping booleans, or returning empty arrays/strings causes at least one test to fail. If a mutation 'survives', the test suite has a blind spot.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Chasing 100% mutation score (some mutations are equivalent — not killable)
Running Stryker on every PR (it's slow; weekly or per-module is sufficient)
Ignoring surviving mutants without justification
Adding assertions just to kill mutants without testing meaningful behavior
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Line coverage tells you code RAN. Mutation testing tells you code's BEHAVIOR is verified. 89% coverage + 41% mutation = tests run code without checking what it does.
2. Equivalent mutants exist (e.g., `i < arr.length` vs `i <= arr.length - 1`). Target 80% mutation score, document the ~20% equivalent ones with justification.
3. Stryker is slow (runs the whole suite once per mutant). Run nightly on main, or per-module on demand — not per-PR.
4. The killing of a mutant is the spec: 'when this operator is wrong, this test must fail' is documentation of what the test guards.
5. Common survivors: error-message strings, log statements, off-by-one in pagination, default values that 'happen to be' the same as the test data.
mock_strategy: Mutation testing uses the existing test suite as-is — no special mocking. The point is to evaluate what the existing tests prove.
edge_cases_tested: Boundary mutations (>, >=) require exact-boundary test cases
Conditional negation (if(x) → if(!x)) requires positive AND negative cases
Return value mutations (return x → return undefined) require return value assertions
Logical operator swaps (&& → ||) require cases where they diverge
what_would_break_without_this_test: Without mutation testing: 89% line coverage gives false confidence. Real bug: `if(user.role === 'admin')` was refactored to `if(user.role !== 'admin')`, line coverage stayed at 100%, every test still passed because no test exercised a non-admin user. Stryker would have killed that mutation immediately.
ci_considerations: Slow: ~10min for a 500-test suite. Run nightly on main + on-demand via workflow_dispatch. Block PR merge only if mutation score drops > 3% from baseline.

title: Test data factories that don't drift from production
scenario_context: Tests use hand-rolled fixtures: `{id: 'test-1', email: 'a@b.co', createdAt: new Date()}`. Schema added `subscriptionTier` (required); 40 tests still pass with undefined tier because mocks bypass validation. Production breaks for users with missing tier.
what_should_be_tested: That test data is structurally identical to what production produces. Adding a required field to the User type should immediately fail tests using outdated factories, not silently produce malformed objects.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Hand-rolled fixture objects that drift from the schema
Faker without seed (non-reproducible tests)
Factories that bypass the same validators production uses
One giant fixture file shared across the codebase (couples unrelated tests)
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Factories should produce values that PASS the same Zod schema production uses. If the schema requires a field, the factory provides it. Adding a required field = TS error in factory = forced update everywhere.
2. Use a faker with deterministic seed per test for reproducibility. Failing tests give a seed that re-creates the failing data exactly.
3. Factory parameter type is `Partial<T>` so tests override only what they care about. The factory fills the rest with valid defaults — proving 'the property under test' rather than coincidence.
4. Trait-based factories: `userFactory({trait:'admin'})` builds on the base. Better than 10 ad-hoc fixtures.
5. The factory IS tested — at least one assertion that the factory output passes the schema. Prevents factory rot.
mock_strategy: Factory wraps the same Zod schema production uses — single source of truth. No separate validation logic in tests.
edge_cases_tested: Factory validates its own output (catches factory rot)
what_would_break_without_this_test: Real incident: User got `phoneNumber` as a new required field. Hand-rolled fixtures didn't include it. Validation in production rejected sign-ups; tests passed because they constructed `{...} as User`. Factory-with-schema-parse would have failed immediately when `phoneNumber` was added.
ci_considerations: Factory call adds ~0.5ms (Zod parse). Negligible. Seed reset per test ensures reproducibility — failing test logs the seed used for that case.

title: Mocking time with a Clock abstraction, not vi.useFakeTimers everywhere
scenario_context: Notification scheduler triggers emails 24h after signup. Tests use `vi.useFakeTimers()` + `vi.advanceTimersByTime(24*60*60*1000)` scattered across 18 test files. Forgetting `vi.useRealTimers()` in afterEach leaks fake time into the next test, causing flaky failures in unrelated suites.
what_should_be_tested: That time-dependent business logic produces correct outputs at specific instants, without globally hijacking the timer system. The production code accepts a Clock; tests pass a FakeClock.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: vi.useFakeTimers() in beforeEach without matching vi.useRealTimers() in afterEach
Calling Date.now() directly in business logic (untestable without globals)
Mocking the Date constructor (breaks unrelated libraries)
Sleeping in tests with setTimeout (non-deterministic)
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Clock-as-dependency is more testable than vi.useFakeTimers. You inject the time source, you control it explicitly. No global state, no afterEach traps.
2. Production gets SystemClock, tests get FakeClock. The boundary is clean and visible in function signatures.
3. vi.useFakeTimers is fine for testing timer-using code (setTimeout, setInterval). For 'what does X return at time Y', Clock injection is cleaner.
4. Bug it prevents: a test forgot vi.useRealTimers(). Next test in the worker ran with fake timers, setTimeout never fired, test timed out after 5s with cryptic error.
5. Clock interface is minimal: now(), maybe sleep(). Adding more (Date math, formatting) defeats the purpose — keep it boundary-thin.
mock_strategy: Clock injected at call site. No global timer mocking. SystemClock is the only place Date.now() exists; everything else takes a Clock.
edge_cases_tested: No state leak between tests (each constructs its own FakeClock)
what_would_break_without_this_test: vi.useFakeTimers leak: a scheduler test in suite A forgot useRealTimers. Suite B's HTTP test used `await new Promise(r=>setTimeout(r,10))` which never resolved with fake timers active. Flake rate 12% with cryptic timeout errors. Clock injection has no global state to leak.
ci_considerations: Zero global side effects — tests parallelize trivially. No afterEach cleanup needed. Runtime ~1ms per test.

title: Visual regression that catches real changes without false positives from font rendering
scenario_context: Marketing site uses Playwright screenshots for visual regression. 30% of PRs trigger false-positive diffs from sub-pixel font rendering differences between developer Macs and Linux CI. Team starts ignoring the diffs, defeating the purpose.
what_should_be_tested: That intentional visual changes (new layout, color, spacing) are detected, while non-intentional rendering differences (font hinting, sub-pixel AA) are tolerated. Diffs that matter, not diffs that don't.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Per-developer baselines (drift)
threshold=0 (catches every sub-pixel AA difference)
threshold=0.5 (misses real regressions)
Screenshotting full page including animated banner (always different)
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Baselines must be generated in CI (Linux Chromium), not on developer machines. Devs run --update-snapshots locally only after seeing the CI diff.
2. Mask volatile regions: animated banners, ads, timestamps, user avatars — replace with solid color before capture.
3. maxDiffPixelRatio with a small threshold (0.01 = 1%) tolerates font AA without missing layout shifts.
4. Wait for fonts AND animations: page.evaluate(() => document.fonts.ready) + page.waitForLoadState('networkidle') + disable CSS animations.
5. Pin Chromium version in CI to a specific Playwright release; floating versions cause spurious diffs on browser updates.
mock_strategy: No mocks — the point is real rendering. Network is real (cached via Playwright's cache), DOM is real, CSS is real. Only volatile data sources are masked at the visual layer.
what_would_break_without_this_test: Without masking + threshold tuning: 30% false positive rate → team disables visual regression. With proper config: real layout shift from a Tailwind padding change is caught, while font AA noise is filtered. Real-world: a `gap-4 → gap-3` typo broke pricing grid on mobile; threshold-based diff caught the 80px layout shift while ignoring 200 1-pixel AA differences.
ci_considerations: Baselines stored in repo (~200KB total for a marketing site). Diffs uploaded as CI artifacts for visual review in failed runs. Pinned Playwright version means baseline stability across months.

title: Contract testing API responses with Zod at the boundary
scenario_context: Mobile + web clients call /api/orders. Server adds a new optional field; mobile crashes because it parsed responses with a stale schema. Tests never caught this because they asserted on individual fields, not whole shapes.
what_should_be_tested: That every API response matches the published contract (Zod schema), and that the schema is the SAME one clients import. Adding/removing fields requires schema change, breaking the test until clients are updated.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Asserting on individual fields (`expect(r.body.id).toBe(...)`) — misses extras/removals
Separate server and client schemas (drift)
Loose schemas (.passthrough() everywhere) — defeats the purpose
Changing schemas without versioning
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Schema lives in a shared package (`@yourorg/api-contracts`). Server validates outgoing responses against it; clients parse incoming responses with it. One source of truth.
2. Tests use schema.parse() (strict by default) — any field the schema doesn't know about throws. Forces explicit decisions on every field.
3. For optional new fields: bump minor version, both sides update. For removed/renamed fields: bump major, with deprecation period.
4. Catches a class of bugs: server returns `userId` instead of `user_id`, server forgets to include `total`, server adds undocumented debug field that breaks mobile parser.
5. Faster than full E2E, more rigorous than 'check status 200'.
mock_strategy: Real DB for the data, real Express for the routing. Schema is the contract — no mocking it.
edge_cases_tested: Strict mode rejects unknown fields (server can't sneak debug data in)
All enum values produce valid responses
Optional fields handled (missing OK, present must validate)
what_would_break_without_this_test: Mobile crash: server added `internalDebugInfo` field; mobile's Codable model crashed on unknown key. With contract test, server-side schema rejects unknown field before response leaves the building.
ci_considerations: Adds ~5ms per contract test (Zod parse). Run on every PR. Schema change = cross-team coordination signal, exactly what you want.

title: Testing a custom hook with renderHook and act
scenario_context: useDebounced<T>(value, ms) returns the latest value after `ms` of stability. Bug shipped: rapid changes within the debounce window canceled correctly, BUT changing `ms` mid-flight didn't restart the timer, leading to early fires.
what_should_be_tested: That the hook returns the initial value immediately, suppresses changes within the window, emits the latest after stability, and properly restarts when `ms` changes mid-flight.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Calling the hook outside renderHook (works but bypasses React's update batching)
forgetting act() around state updates (warnings, false passes)
Using setTimeout in tests instead of vi.useFakeTimers (non-deterministic)
Testing every internal useState — test the hook's RETURN value
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. renderHook gives you a controlled mount/rerender lifecycle. result.current reflects the latest render.
2. Fake timers + act() let you advance time and flush React updates deterministically.
3. Test what the hook EMITS (its return value), not how (no peering at useRef internals).
4. The ms-change bug: rerender with a different ms didn't reset the existing timer. Test: rerender with new ms, advance by OLD ms, value should NOT have updated yet.
5. Cleanup must work: unmount during the debounce window shouldn't fire after unmount (memory leak / setState-on-unmounted warning).
mock_strategy: vi.useFakeTimers() because the hook DEPENDS on setTimeout — exactly what fake timers exist for. React's act() flushes pending state updates after time advance.
what_would_break_without_this_test: The ms-change bug: a search input switched debounce based on user typing speed. Without the regression test, changing the debounce mid-type triggered an early fire with stale value, sending the wrong API request. Caught on the rerender-with-new-ms test.
ci_considerations: <10ms per test. Deterministic via fake timers. Parallel-safe (each test has own renderer).

title: Per-worker Postgres schemas for safe parallel integration tests
scenario_context: Integration suite has 300 tests against Postgres. Running serial: 8 minutes. Sharing one DB across workers: 50% failure rate from collisions on global state (sequences, advisory locks). Per-worker databases: slow setup (10s × N workers).
what_should_be_tested: Same business logic, but parallelized safely. Each worker operates in isolation (no shared tables) without paying the cost of bootstrapping a whole DB per worker.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Sharing one schema with `TRUNCATE` between tests (slow + serialized)
Per-worker databases (slow startup, connection storms)
Hard-coded schema names (collide on re-runs)
Forgetting to drop the schema after the run (DB bloat)
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. One Postgres instance, N schemas (one per worker). Each connection sets `SET search_path TO worker_<id>` at acquisition.
2. Schema creation = milliseconds, schema drop = milliseconds. Way faster than CREATE DATABASE.
3. Migrations run once per schema in globalSetup, not per test.
4. Vitest exposes `process.env.VITEST_POOL_ID` per worker — use it as the schema suffix.
5. Connection pool is per-worker too, so `search_path` doesn't get reset by other workers.
mock_strategy: Real Postgres. Per-worker schema isolation removes the need for mocking shared state.
edge_cases_tested: Worker N+1 doesn't see worker N's rows
Re-run after crash recreates schema (DROP IF EXISTS)
what_would_break_without_this_test: Shared DB: tests on `users` table collided. Per-DB workers: 6 workers × 10s startup = 60s overhead, connection storm exhausted Postgres max_connections. Per-schema: 6 workers × 0.3s = 1.8s overhead, 1 Postgres instance.
ci_considerations: 6× parallelism, 8min → 90s. Postgres handles up to ~50 schemas comfortably; reuse instance across CI jobs.

title: Network mocking via route handlers for deterministic E2E
scenario_context: E2E test 'shows error when payment fails' depends on a real flaky test gateway. 12% flake rate. Need deterministic failure injection without losing the E2E value of testing the real client.
what_should_be_tested: The UI flow when the payment API returns 402 — error banner, retry button enabled, form not reset. Mock JUST the payment endpoint, leave everything else real.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Mocking everything (then it's not E2E)
Modifying the dev server to inject errors (couples test to server)
Triggering failure via test-only UI buttons (test-only code in prod bundle)
Mocking without asserting the request the app made
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. page.route() intercepts at the browser network layer. App runs unmodified — exactly the same code as prod.
2. Mock the EXTERNAL boundary (payment gateway), not internal endpoints. Tests still validate your own backend.
3. Assert on the request payload — confirms the app sent what it should before mocking the response.
4. Restore route after test or scope to test() block so other tests aren't affected.
5. Latency simulation (route.fulfill with delay) catches loading-state bugs.
mock_strategy: Only the external boundary (payment) is mocked. Your backend, your DB, your auth — all real. Pattern: mock at the edge of YOUR system.
edge_cases_tested: Retry button enabled (not stuck in submitting state)
what_would_break_without_this_test: Without route mocking: 12% flake from real gateway. With route mocking: 100% deterministic. Real bug caught: clicking 'Pay' second time after error sent the request twice — race against the disabled state. Test exposed it because mocked endpoint logged 2 hits.
ci_considerations: Mocked endpoints add zero network latency unless intentional. Suite ran 4× faster + zero flake. Per-test route scoping prevents leak.

title: Model-based testing for a shopping cart state machine
scenario_context: Cart logic has invariants no example test fully covers: totalCents == sum of line item totals, qty never < 1 after add, removing last item leaves empty cart not empty line. Edge cases keep slipping through example tests.
what_should_be_tested: That an arbitrary sequence of commands (add, remove, updateQty, clear) preserves the invariants. Generate random valid sequences, run against the real implementation AND a simple reference model, check they agree.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Hand-writing all sequences (you'll miss the bad ones)
Reference model that's as complex as the real one (just duplicates bugs)
Properties so weak they pass on broken code
Ignoring shrinking — debugging a 500-step counter-example by hand
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Model-based testing: a simple, obviously-correct reference (e.g., Map<sku,qty>) is the spec. Random commands run against both real and model; outputs should match.
2. Shrinks to minimal failing sequence. Often surprises: 'add A, add A, removeBySku A → cart has -1 quantity'.
3. Commands are typed values — fast-check generates only valid sequences.
4. Catches what example tests miss: ordering effects, idempotency violations, accumulator bugs.
5. Reference model is INTENTIONALLY simpler than the real impl (no batching, no events) — that's the point.
mock_strategy: Zero mocks. Reference model IS the spec; real impl is tested against it.
edge_cases_tested: updateQty(0) treated as remove (idempotency with remove)
Clear after any sequence yields empty
what_would_break_without_this_test: Real bug: updateQty(sku, 0) decremented to 0 but kept the line. Total was right; line count was wrong. Property test 'lines.every(qty>=1)' caught it in 12 runs with a 3-command counter-example: add A, add B, updateQty A 0.
ci_considerations: 500 runs × 2 properties ≈ 200ms. Seed fixed for reproducibility; print seed on failure for ad-hoc debug.

title: Snapshot testing done right: small, intentional, reviewed
scenario_context: Component snapshot suite has 200 snapshots, mostly auto-updated on diff. PRs include `snap update` commits without review. Real regression in a heading hierarchy shipped because snapshot was auto-accepted.
what_should_be_tested: That specific, stable outputs match a curated snapshot. NOT 'the whole component renders this big tree'. Snapshots prove intent; if it changes, a human reviews WHY.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Snapshotting entire component trees (200-line snapshots, nobody reads diffs)
Auto-updating snapshots on every CI run
toMatchSnapshot on dynamic content (dates, uuids)
Snapshots as the only test (they say 'output looks the same', not 'behavior is correct')
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Snapshots should be small enough to read in a PR diff. If you can't visually parse it, you can't review it.
2. Use inline snapshots (toMatchInlineSnapshot) — the expected value lives next to the assertion. Reviewer sees both at once.
3. Snapshot serializers strip volatility (dates, IDs) so diffs reflect real changes only.
4. Snapshot output, not implementation: e.g., the accessibility tree, the rendered text content, the className list — not the full HTML.
5. Snapshots COMPLEMENT explicit assertions, they don't replace them. Snapshot says 'shape is stable'; assertions say 'behavior is correct'.
mock_strategy: No mocks. Snapshot serializer normalizes volatility (timestamps, UUIDs) so diffs reflect intent.
edge_cases_tested: Total computed correctly (explicit assertion, not snapshot)
what_would_break_without_this_test:
Full-tree snapshots get rubber-stamped on update. With small targeted snapshots, a change from `

Total

` to `
Total
` (accidental heading downgrade, bad for screen readers) shows up as a 2-line diff that's obvious in review.
ci_considerations: CI fails on snapshot mismatch (no --ci-update). Updating snapshots requires explicit local run + manual commit + review.

title: Testing rate limiting without sleeping in tests
scenario_context: Express middleware caps /api/login at 5 req/min per IP. Tests use real timers + Promise sleep, take 60s+ to verify the limit resets. Suite avoids them; the bug-prone middleware ships untested.
what_should_be_tested: That 6th request within window returns 429, that successful auth doesn't count against the limit, that limit resets after the window, that different IPs are independent. Without real-time delays.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: await sleep(60_000) — locks up CI
Mocking the rate limiter itself (then you don't test it)
Hard-coded IPs that conflict in CI
Race conditions from concurrent test runs sharing the limiter state
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. The limiter takes a Clock dependency (same trick as test-009). Tests advance the clock instead of waiting.
2. Test the BEHAVIOR (429 after N, reset after window) — not internal counter values.
3. Test isolation: each test constructs its own limiter instance with a fresh Clock and store.
4. Distributed rate limiter (Redis-backed) tests use a real Redis on a per-test prefix.
5. Race condition test: parallel requests should be counted accurately (test with Promise.all).
mock_strategy: Clock is injected (FakeClock). Store is real in-memory map. Redis-backed store would be tested separately against real Redis.
edge_cases_tested: Boundary: exactly N allowed, N+1 blocked
what_would_break_without_this_test: Tests skipped entirely → an off-by-one made the limiter allow N+1 instead of N. Bug shipped. With Clock injection, suite runs in 15ms and covers all behaviors deterministically.
ci_considerations: ~15ms total. Zero real time waiting. Parallel-safe — each test owns its app + clock.

title: Testing error boundaries with intentional throw
scenario_context: App wraps each route in <ErrorBoundary fallback={...}/>. Bug shipped: when a child threw during render, boundary swallowed the error but didn't reset on route change, leaving 'Something went wrong' permanently displayed.
what_should_be_tested: That a throwing child renders the fallback; that the boundary's `reset()` allows recovery; that changing route prop triggers reset; that the error is reported to error tracking.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: console.error spam during tests (use console.error spy or onError config)
Throwing in useEffect (boundaries catch render errors, not effect errors)
Forgetting to test the recovery path (only happy-error path)
Asserting on the error message instead of the user-facing fallback
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. ErrorBoundary catches errors during render/commit, NOT in event handlers or effects. Test must throw at render time.
2. React 19 still logs caught errors to console.error — suppress in tests so logs aren't noisy and don't trigger CI noise-detection.
3. Recovery test is essential: a boundary that catches but never recovers is a 'forever broken' UI.
4. Spy on the error reporting callback — boundary should still notify Sentry/your tracker.
5. resetKeys prop pattern: reset when a key (like pathname) changes. The bug fix.
mock_strategy: console.error spy to suppress noise. onError prop is a vi.fn() to assert reporting. No mocking of React itself.
edge_cases_tested: onError called with the actual error
what_would_break_without_this_test: The 'forever broken UI' bug: route changed, app navigated underneath, but ErrorBoundary's internal state still had the old error. resetKeys test would have caught it pre-merge.
ci_considerations: ~30ms per test. console.error spy ensures clean CI logs; restored in afterEach so other tests aren't affected.

title: Mobile viewport testing with device emulation
scenario_context: Site looks great on desktop, breaks on iPhone SE (375px wide) because of a sidebar that doesn't collapse. No mobile testing in CI. Users complain via support tickets.
what_should_be_tested: That key flows work on real mobile viewports. Touch interactions (tap, swipe), reduced screen real estate, mobile-specific UI (hamburger menu, bottom sheets) all function correctly.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Resizing only viewport but keeping desktop user-agent (responsive but no touch events)
Testing only the smallest viewport (iPad-sized bugs slip through)
Using Playwright's deviceScaleFactor without proper touch emulation
Forgetting that mobile Safari has unique quirks (100vh issue, etc.)
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Playwright's devices preset gives realistic device emulation: viewport + UA + hasTouch + isMobile + scale factor.
2. Test at the inflection points: iPhone SE (smallest common), iPhone 14 (modal mid-size), iPad Mini (tablet), iPad Pro landscape (desktop-ish but touch).
3. Touch events trigger different code paths than mouse (tap() vs click()).
4. Mobile bugs are often layout (overflow-x scroll, hamburger menu visibility) — covered by combining behavior tests with viewport-targeted screenshots.
5. Project per device — same specs run in matrix, no per-test viewport juggling.
mock_strategy: No mocks at this layer — real rendering, real touch events. App backend mocked separately if needed for state setup.
edge_cases_tested: Hamburger menu present on mobile, hidden on desktop
No horizontal overflow at smallest viewport
Touch interactions trigger correctly (tap vs click)
Tablet inflection: desktop-sized but isMobile flag may be touch-enabled
what_would_break_without_this_test: iPhone SE sidebar bug shipped because all tests ran at 1440px. CI matrix across 4 devices catches device-specific layout breaks pre-merge.
ci_considerations: 4 projects × N tests = 4x runtime, but parallelized across workers. Use shard count = device count × 2 for balanced load.

title: Splitting E2E tests across CI shards for parallel execution
scenario_context: E2E suite has 180 tests, takes 22 minutes serial. Need to fit in 5-minute CI budget. Naive sharding (split by file count) leaves shard 3 with all the slow login flows; finishes 11 min while others finish in 2.
what_should_be_tested: Same suite, parallelized intelligently — shards balanced by duration not file count, results aggregated, retries only what's needed. Suite stays under budget without flakiness.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Random sharding (one shard ends up with all the slow tests)
Sharding by alphabetical file order (couples runtime to filename luck)
Per-shard browser install (slow, redundant)
Aggregating shard reports manually
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Playwright's --shard X/Y is round-robin file-based. For balanced shards, use a timing report from previous runs to assign files to shards by total duration.
2. Browser binary cached at OS level (Playwright's cache dir is stable per version). Shards reuse it.
3. Blob reporter outputs per-shard results; merge job aggregates into one HTML report.
4. Retries on test, not shard: failing tests retry inside the same shard (--retries=2), not by re-running the whole shard.
5. Sticky-shard pattern: cache the timing report between CI runs; rebalance weekly.
mock_strategy: N/A — infrastructure setup. Cache strategy ensures browser install doesn't run per-shard.
edge_cases_tested: Shard failure doesn't fail the whole job (continue-on-error per shard, merge report shows all)
Test failure retries within shard (not whole shard re-run)
Browser cache hit (skip 80MB download per shard)
what_would_break_without_this_test: 22-minute suite with naive sharding stays at 11 min (slowest shard). Duration-balanced sharding gets each shard to ~4 min. 6 shards × 4 min in parallel = budget met.
ci_considerations: 6× parallelism, blob reporter aggregates cleanly, browser binary cached. Total wall-clock: ~5 min instead of 22 min serial.

title: Contract testing between microservices with Pact
scenario_context: Orders service depends on Inventory service's HTTP API. When Inventory deploys a breaking schema change (rename `available` → `availableQty`), Orders breaks in production despite both having green test suites.
what_should_be_tested: That Orders' expectations of Inventory match what Inventory actually delivers — at build time, not in production. Consumer publishes a contract; provider verifies it can satisfy it before deploying.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: E2E tests that boot both services (slow, brittle)
Mocking the provider in consumer tests without verifying mocks against reality
Sharing TypeScript types across services (couples deploys; defeats microservices boundary)
Consumer-driven contract without enforcement in provider CI
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Consumer-driven contracts: Orders declares 'this is what I need from Inventory'. Pact records interactions during consumer tests. Provider runs verification: 'can my code satisfy these recorded interactions?'
2. Breaks the deploy chain: Inventory's CI fails BEFORE deploy if breaking a contract a consumer depends on.
3. Pact broker hosts contracts; both sides query it. can-i-deploy gate prevents incompatible deploys.
4. Versioning: contracts tagged by consumer version, so v1 of Orders verifies against v2 of Inventory while v2 of Orders is still in dev.
5. Catches schema drift, field rename, type changes, removed endpoints — every cause of the 'works on my machine, broken in prod' microservices class.
mock_strategy: Pact provides the mock server during consumer tests. Provider tests use real server + real DB (with state handlers). Mocks are recorded and verified — they can't drift unchecked.
edge_cases_tested: Field rename in provider fails consumer's contract verification
Provider state handlers seed exact DB state consumer expects
can-i-deploy blocks deploys that would break a consumer in any environment
what_would_break_without_this_test: The available→availableQty rename: Orders' tests passed (mocked old shape), Inventory's tests passed (returned new shape). Pact verification fails on the contract published by Orders. Inventory's deploy is blocked until a coordinated rollout strategy is chosen.
ci_considerations: Provider verification: ~30-60s. Consumer pact generation: <5s. Pact broker hosted separately (Pactflow or self-hosted). can-i-deploy as a deploy-pipeline gate, not a test.

title: Coverage thresholds that mean something (not 100%-or-bust)
scenario_context: Team mandates 100% coverage. Result: 40% of tests are `it('exists',()=>expect(fn).toBeDefined())`. Real bugs ship. Coverage badge stays green.
what_should_be_tested: Critical business logic at high coverage (>90%), boundaries/glue code at moderate (>70%), generated code/types excluded. Coverage is a signal, not a target.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Single global threshold (forces meaningless tests for trivial code)
100% line coverage as quality gate (incentivizes assertion-free tests)
Including generated files, types, config in coverage
Coverage on test files themselves
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Tiered thresholds per directory: src/domain (business rules) → 90%, src/ui → 70%, src/adapters → 60%, src/types → excluded.
2. Branch coverage > line coverage for revealing untested logic.
3. Coverage thresholds should be a FLOOR (don't drop below), not a target. Mutation score is the quality measure.
4. Exclude: .d.ts, generated/, *.config.ts, src/index.ts (re-exports), migrations.
5. CI publishes coverage delta on PR — flagging drops, not absolute numbers.
mock_strategy: N/A — configuration. v8 provider is faster than istanbul and works with TS source maps natively.
edge_cases_tested: Excludes prevent coverage inflation from generated/types
Branch coverage forces testing both sides of conditionals
Delta check catches regressions PRs would otherwise hide
what_would_break_without_this_test: 100% target: tests like `expect(fn).toBeDefined()` exist solely to bump coverage. Real bugs slip because nobody asserts behavior. Tiered + delta + mutation = quality signal that resists gaming.
ci_considerations: v8 coverage adds ~10-15% to test runtime. Delta check is sub-second. Reports posted to PR as comment.

title: Testing Context providers without re-render explosions
scenario_context: AuthContext re-renders all consumers on any state change (login → re-render every memoized component). Tests don't catch this because they assert state, not render counts. Production: 200+ wasted re-renders on every auth event.
what_should_be_tested: That context consumers re-render ONLY when their slice changes. Split contexts (state vs dispatch), or use a selector pattern, and verify with a render-count tracker that other slices don't trigger re-renders.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Single AuthContext with both user data + setters (any change re-renders everyone)
useContext returning a new object every render (causes infinite re-renders)
Testing 'context provides value' without testing re-render granularity
Asserting render count without resetting between assertions
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Two-context pattern: AuthStateContext (immutable per session) + AuthDispatchContext (callable, stable reference). Consumers subscribe to what they need.
2. Test with a render counter: a component that increments a ref counter on every render. Assert the counter stays at 1 when an unrelated slice updates.
3. React.memo + reference-stable context value prevents cascading re-renders. Test verifies the contract.
4. The 200-renders-on-login bug: every consumer of AuthContext re-rendered because the context value was a new object. Split fixes it; test enforces it.
5. Pattern transfers to Redux/Zustand selectors, observable state, etc.
mock_strategy: No mocks. Render counter via ref is the assertion vehicle.
edge_cases_tested: Memoized child consuming only dispatch doesn't re-render on state change
Dispatch reference stability (memoized children correctly skip)
what_would_break_without_this_test: Single-context anti-pattern shipped: 200+ memoized components re-rendered on login. Page froze ~80ms on auth event. Two-context split fixed it; render-count test prevents regression on future refactors.
ci_considerations: <20ms per test. Render counts deterministic via React's synchronous rendering in jsdom.

title: Debugging flaky tests with trace viewer and step-through
scenario_context: A test 'checkout completes' fails 4% of CI runs with `Locator click timed out`. Local re-runs pass. Team reflexively retries, masking a real race condition between cart count badge update and submit button enablement.
what_should_be_tested: The full interaction sequence with traceability into WHY a test failed. The trace must capture network, DOM snapshots, console, and screenshots at every step — so flakes become diagnosable.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Increasing timeout to mask the race (bug still exists, just slower)
test.skip on flaky tests (problem hidden, not fixed)
Adding waitForTimeout (works locally, flaky in CI under load)
Increasing retries to 5 (CI passes, prod still broken)
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Playwright's trace captures DOM snapshots, network, console, screenshots per step. The trace viewer shows EXACTLY what state existed when the timeout occurred.
2. trace:'on-first-retry' is the sweet spot — overhead only when needed, full data when flake hits.
3. Web-first assertions (expect(locator).toBeEnabled()) auto-retry up to timeout — that's the right tool. .click() doesn't poll for enablement; it polls for presence.
4. The race: cart count updates triggered re-render that briefly disabled submit. Test caught it 4% of the time. Fix: assert button is ENABLED before clicking, not just visible.
5. Trace-as-CI-artifact: download from failed run, open with `npx playwright show-trace`, see the exact race.
mock_strategy: None at the diagnosis layer. Trace records the real interaction; fix is to wait for the right state assertion, not mock it away.
edge_cases_tested: Race between cart-update re-render and submit-enabled state
Retries don't mask real bugs (trace from first failure preserved)
what_would_break_without_this_test: Without trace + the toBeEnabled() assertion: 4% flake rate, retries pass, prod has same race on slow connections. With proper wait + trace: race surfaces in CI as a deterministic failure pointing exactly at the submit-button state mismatch.
ci_considerations: Trace on retry adds ~2MB per failed test (gzipped). Negligible storage. Diagnostic value: hours saved debugging vs guessing.

title: Testing webhook handlers with signature verification
scenario_context: Stripe webhook handler receives POST /webhooks/stripe. Production bug: an attacker discovered the endpoint, sent fake `charge.succeeded` events, our system credited $0 charges. Signature verification was 'TODO' in the handler.
what_should_be_tested: That requests with INVALID signatures are rejected (401), valid signatures are accepted, replay attacks (old timestamp) are rejected, AND the business logic processes events correctly. Webhooks are an attack surface; verification is non-negotiable.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Skipping signature verification 'for testing' (creates the very bug we ship)
Hardcoding webhook secret in test files
Testing the happy path only
Trusting body content without verification
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Signature verification is the test target. Use stripe's library helpers (or matching algorithm) to construct valid + invalid signatures.
2. Test the timestamp window: events older than 5 minutes are rejected (replay defense).
3. Idempotency test: receiving the same event twice processes it once (Stripe retries).
4. Body parsing order matters: signature is over the RAW body. If you JSON.parse before signature check, verification fails.
5. Real bug: the original handler ran express.json() globally, mangling the body before signature check. Test must use raw body.
mock_strategy: Clock injected (replay test needs deterministic time). processEvent is vi.fn() — verifies handler called/not-called per signature outcome. Crypto is real — verifying the verifier.
edge_cases_tested: Duplicate event id processed once (Stripe retry resilience)
what_would_break_without_this_test: The $0-charge fraud: skipped signature verification. With these tests in place, the unverified handler would never pass CI. The timestamp test prevents replay. Idempotency test prevents double-credit on Stripe retries.
ci_considerations: ~50ms per test. Crypto is fast. No external services.

title: Property test for a JSON serializer round-trip
scenario_context: Custom JSON serializer for Money/Decimal types. Round-tripping (encode then decode) should produce the original value. Example tests cover happy cases; production bug: very small decimals (1e-9) lose precision through the round trip.
what_should_be_tested: For any valid Money value M, decode(encode(M)) === M. Across the entire valid input space — large numbers, tiny decimals, negative values, edge currencies.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Only testing values you generated in the encoder ('decoder agrees with itself' — meaningless)
Asserting equality on float results without epsilon (passes on Math.PI but lies about precision)
Properties that pass for null/undefined inputs (skip-rather-than-fail anti-pattern)
Ignoring the shrinker — debugging a 20-decimal-place counterexample by hand
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Round-trip is the canonical serializer property: ∀x: decode(encode(x)) ≡ x.
2. Use bigint internally to avoid float drift — property test verifies the encoded JSON string parses back to the exact same bigint.
3. fast-check generates extreme values: max safe bigint, negative, zero, single-cent amounts, max precision.
4. The 1e-9 bug: encoder used Number() conversion before stringifying, losing precision. Property test with arbitrary bigints catches it immediately, shrinks to minimal failing amount.
5. Bonus property: encode(x) is always valid JSON (JSON.parse succeeds).
mock_strategy: None. Pure serialization. Property tests are the spec.
what_would_break_without_this_test: Number() conversion in encoder loses precision above 2^53. Bug missed by examples because nobody wrote a $90 trillion test. Property test failed on first run with shrunk counter-example like `{amount:9007199254740993n,scale:0,currency:'USD'}`.
ci_considerations: 1000 runs × 2 props ≈ 60ms. Seed printed on failure. Run on every PR.

title: Killing surviving mutants in a date-range utility
scenario_context: isDateInRange(date, start, end) is well-tested by example. Stryker reports 73% mutation score; surviving mutants include `>= → >` on the start boundary and `<= → <` on the end. The 'inclusive on both ends' rule isn't actually tested.
what_should_be_tested: That the boundaries are EXACTLY as specified: start inclusive, end inclusive. Mutations to the operators must fail at least one test.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Boundary tests that use 'middle' values that don't pinpoint the operator
Tests with redundant data (same boundary tested 5 ways)
Killing mutants by adding meaningless assertions (kills the mutant but doesn't test behavior)
Accepting a mutant as 'equivalent' without checking — most aren't
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Boundary mutations (>, >=, <, <=) survive when no test uses an EXACT-boundary value.
2. Add tests at start, start-1ms, end, end+1ms. The 1ms delta pins the operator.
3. Document WHY the boundary tests exist (kills specific mutants) so future devs don't 'simplify' them.
4. The 73% → 92% lift comes from 4 added tests. High-signal, low-cost.
5. Equivalent mutants (e.g., `if(!arr.length) return [...arr]`) get a `// stryker disable next-line` with a comment explaining why.
mock_strategy: None. Pure function.
what_would_break_without_this_test: A refactor 'simplifies' the comparison to `> && <`. Without boundary tests, all happy-path tests pass; production silently excludes events at the exact range boundaries (like end-of-month reports). Stryker would flag the surviving operator mutation before merge.
ci_considerations: Boundary tests are <1ms each. Mutation score lift 73→92% with 4 lines of test. Run Stryker nightly to catch new surviving mutants.

title: Deterministic test order without coupling tests
scenario_context: Vitest runs files in parallel and tests in arbitrary order within a file. Suite has flaky test 'admin can promote user' that passes alone, fails after 'admin can demote user' because of shared in-memory state in a singleton.
what_should_be_tested: That every test passes regardless of order. The test runner should randomize order to surface order dependencies AS BUGS, not pretend stability with fixed order.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: test.serial / test.concurrent flags to mask order coupling
Singletons holding state across tests
Global module-level state (e.g., `const cache = {}` at top of impl file)
Fixed order in test config (hides the bug, doesn't fix it)
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Random test order surfaces ordering bugs. Vitest's sequence.shuffle=true does this. Seed printed on failure → reproducible.
2. Test isolation must come from PROPER lifecycle hooks (beforeEach reset), NOT from order. Singletons need a reset() method called per test.
3. The fix is to find the shared state, expose a reset hook, call it in beforeEach. NOT to add test.serial.
4. Pure functions are order-independent by construction. The smell is mutable module state.
5. Catches a class of 'works on Monday, fails on Tuesday' bugs — different CI shard, different order, intermittent failures.
mock_strategy: Reset hook on the singleton is the seam. Don't mock the singleton — that hides the bug.
edge_cases_tested: Each test passes after any other test (order-independent)
Singleton state explicitly cleared between tests
Failure reproducible from the printed seed
what_would_break_without_this_test: Without shuffle: tests pass in author-order, fail when CI runs a different shard order. Shuffled: bug surfaces immediately, fix lives in beforeEach. Fix without shuffle: bug pops up months later in a refactor.
ci_considerations: Shuffle adds <1ms. Logging seed enables reproduction on failure. Bug-find rate increases dramatically on legacy codebases.

title: Testing accessibility with axe-core
scenario_context: Sign-up form passes manual review but fails screen reader users: input labels associated by sibling proximity, not htmlFor. Lighthouse a11y score 100 (it doesn't catch this). User research surfaces it after launch.
what_should_be_tested: That rendered components have no automated a11y violations. Run axe-core in component tests so violations fail builds, not customer support tickets.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Manual a11y review as the only check (slow, easy to skip)
Testing component renders without testing the a11y of the render
axe in E2E only (caught later, fixes are more expensive)
Disabling axe rules without writing why
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. axe-core finds ~30% of WCAG issues automatically — high-ROI quick check.
2. Run in component tests: fast feedback, fix while you're in the file.
3. jest-axe (or vitest-axe) integrates as a matcher: `expect(await axe(container)).toHaveNoViolations()`.
4. Per-violation, the report names the rule and the element — fix is obvious.
5. The 30% automated + 70% manual is the right split. Don't pretend axe catches everything.
mock_strategy: None — axe runs against the real rendered DOM.
edge_cases_tested: Label association via htmlFor + useId (stable IDs)
what_would_break_without_this_test: Sibling-proximity labels: screen readers announce the input with no label. Users hear 'edit, blank' instead of 'email, edit text'. Form unusable. axe catches it as `label` rule violation in the test, not in production user research.
ci_considerations: axe adds ~50-100ms per call. Run on key components, not every render. Block PR on new violations.

title: Test naming as executable documentation
scenario_context: Test file: `it('test1',...)`, `it('works',...)`, `it('handles errors',...)`. New engineer reads it, learns nothing about what the system does. Bug fix changes a test, nobody reviews because the name doesn't say what behavior is at stake.
what_should_be_tested: Same behaviors — but named so the test file READS like a behavior spec. Reviewer can audit 'do these names describe the system correctly?' without reading the code.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Test names that describe code structure ('renders App component')
Test names with no observable subject ('it works', 'happy path')
Test names that lie (passes but checks the wrong thing)
Generic 'test X' / 'test Y' that don't survive refactor
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Test name = behavior assertion in plain English: SUBJECT + ACTION + EXPECTED OUTCOME.
2. Pattern: 'when CONDITION, OUTPUT' or 'CONDITION → OUTCOME'. Reads as a sentence.
3. describe() groups by feature/component; it() describes a single behavior. Together: `Cart > when empty > checkout button is disabled`.
4. PR reviewer can read just the test names and validate the spec without reading the implementation.
5. Test failure message becomes self-explanatory: 'Cart > when empty > checkout button is disabled — FAIL' tells you the bug in 10 words.
mock_strategy: N/A. Naming convention.
edge_cases_tested: Names cover all behaviors readable as a spec
Reviewer can audit spec from names alone
what_would_break_without_this_test: Opaque names: failing CI says `Cart > test1 FAILED`. Engineer reads code to understand what broke. Multiply by 500 tests in a suite, half from departed engineers — minutes to hours of decode time per failure. Clear names: failure is self-explanatory at a glance.
ci_considerations: Zero runtime impact. Improves PR review time (audit spec from names) and on-call debug time (failure messages tell the story).
